text:
The Olympic Games returns to France from August 26, enjoy the best reports, news and analysis from Paris 2024 with Cyclingnews

When you purchase through links on our site, we may earn an affiliate commission.Here’s how it works.

-

The latest race content, interviews, features, reviews and expert buying guides, direct to your inbox!

You are now subscribed

Your newsletter sign-up was successful

The world’s best cyclists will race in Paris this summer, not at the Tour de France or Tour de France Femmes but for Olympic gold on the roads of the capital, on the Paris velodrome and on the mountain bike and BMX tracks.

Following their Tour de France exploits,Tadej Pogačarand Remco Evenepoel will also race in Paris but face strong opposition in the prestigious road race fromMathieu van der Poel,Wout van Aertand many others though in the 275km road race that is akin to a Spring Classic.

Demi Vollering,Lotte Kopecky, andLorena Wiebeswill lput their pro team friendships to one side as they vie to deliver for their respective nations in the 158km women's road race.

The time trial are the first road events on August 27, with the road races a week later on August 3 and 4.

Some riders will compete in the time trials as a warm-up for the road races, while specialists likeFilippo Gannawill go on to race on the track.

All eyes will be on home favouritePauline Ferrand-Prévotin the women's mountain bike race as she makes a final bid to become Olympic champion before calling time on her fat tyre career. Tokyo mountain bike gold medalistTom Pidcockheadlines a quality field in the men’s event.

Track racing is an Olympic stalwart, having been present at every modern Games apart from the 1912 edition in Stockholm. Six events offer a chance to win a gold medal on the track, with Italy looking to defend their success in the men's team pursuit and defeat Great Britain in the women's team pursuit.

We will bring you around-the-clock coverage, with race reports, interviews, news and  analysis of all the action from the road,mountain biking, andtrackevents at the Olympic Games.



Stephen Farrand and Laura Wieslo will be in Paris for Cyclingnews to report on all the road, track, and mountain biking action at the Olympic Games.

They’ll be supported by theCyclingnewsteam in Europe,North Americaand Australia, ensuring you don’t miss any of the action.

Ahead of the Games, we will interview some of the big names going for Olympic glory, provide detailed explainers on all of the disciplines, and have a go-to hub for everything Paris 2024.

Cyclingnewswill also produce previews and analysis ahead of the key events, alongside our contenders for gold, route breakdowns, and a medal count of the cycling disciplines.

You can enjoy unlimited, complete access to all of our journalism and content by subscribing toCyclingnews.

Our content is initially free to view but once you’ve viewed five articles in a one-month period you will be prompted to take out a digital subscription –you can read more about that here.

A subscription toCyclingnewsallows you to read every word and see every photograph we publish. Your subscription also helps support our journalism, and especially our presence at races across the season, when we bring you the big stories and inside news that nobody else has.

It also gives access to ourpremium contentwhich cuts back on advertising to deliver in-depth journalism - such as exclusive interviews, tech galleries and longer reads - made especially for our subscribers.

Visit ourOlympic Games hub pagefor more information on the 33rd edition of the Games and please joinCyclingnewsfor all the latest news and results from Paris 2024.



Cyclingnews is the world's leader in English-language coverage of professional cycling. Started in 1995 by University of Newcastle professor Bill Mitchell, the site was one of the first to provide breaking news and results over the internet in English. The site was purchased by Knapp Communications in 1999, and owner Gerard Knapp built it into the definitive voice of pro cycling. Since then, major publishing house Future PLC has owned the site and expanded it to include top features, news, results, photos and tech reporting. The site continues to be the most comprehensive and authoritative English voice in professional cycling.

Cyclingnews is part of Future plc, an international media group and leading digital publisher.Visit our corporate site.
